article_title: You need these 6 self-hosted tools if you value your privacy
core_thesis: Self-hosting open-source tools is the best way to regain privacy and control over personal data, replacing cloud services that harvest, sell, or expose user information. The article presents six specific self-hosted applications that address common privacy pain points—file conversion, PDF editing, password management, file syncing, search, and smart home control—while also mentioning additional tools like Nextcloud, Jellyfin, and Immich for a comprehensive privacy-respecting stack.
detailed_summary: The article argues that beyond saving money on subscriptions, the primary benefit of self-hosted FOSS tools is privacy: they run locally, preventing companies from harvesting data for AI training, selling it, or showing ads. The author, Ayush Pande, shares six tools he personally uses to replace privacy-intrusive cloud services. Vert is a local file conversion tool supporting many formats, with an optional FFmpeg wrapper (Vertd) for video conversion. BentoPDF offers full PDF editing (annotations, splitting, metadata changes) without cloud exposure. Vaultwarden is a lightweight, self-hosted Bitwarden-compatible password manager that stores passwords, TOTP codes, passkeys, and more, with client apps retaining offline copies. Syncthing syncs files across devices (Windows, macOS, Linux, FreeBSD) with fine-tuned settings; the author uses it for coding files but notes it's not a backup tool—recommending Rsync or Kopia instead. SearXNG is a metasearch engine that aggregates results from multiple search engines without tracking, and can be paired with Open WebUI for local LLM integration. Home Assistant centralizes smart home device management, avoiding cloud dependency and its security/privacy risks, and enables powerful automations. The article also mentions Nextcloud, Jellyfin, RomM, Immich, Firefly III, Paperless-ngx, and Cryptomator as additional privacy-respecting tools.

entities.name: Ayush Pande
entities.role: Author of the article, PC hardware and gaming writer at XDA

entities.name: Immich
entities.role: Self-hosted Google Photos alternative (mentioned as a bonus)

key_data_points: Vert supports conversion of documents, images, and audio files locally, with video conversion available via Vertd (FFmpeg wrapper) on systems with a decent GPU (🟢 relevant to your homelab: can be deployed on a modest server for efficient batch conversion).

BentoPDF provides full PDF editing capabilities—annotations, splitting, metadata modification, and content changes—without sending files to the cloud (🟢 relevant to your student needs: useful for handling academic PDFs securely).

Vaultwarden is a lightweight Bitwarden-compatible password manager that stores passwords, TOTP codes, passkeys, API keys, bank card details, and identity documents; client apps retain offline copies to prevent lockout if the server goes down (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: can be used to manage credentials for testing environments, and understanding its offline backup mechanism is useful for operational security).

Syncthing is available on Windows, macOS, Linux, FreeBSD, and Illumos, and allows fine-tuned sync settings to prevent accidental overwrites; it is not recommended for backups—Rsync or Kopia are better for that purpose (🟢 relevant to your DevOps/homelab: ideal for syncing code and configs across multiple machines).

SearXNG aggregates results from multiple search engines without tracking user data, and can be integrated with Open WebUI to allow local LLMs to fetch internet results (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: enables private reconnaissance without exposing search patterns to commercial engines).

Home Assistant centralizes smart home device management, avoiding cloud dependency and its associated security risks (outdated firmware, weak encryption, MITM attacks); it supports automations across devices from different manufacturers (🟢 relevant to your homelab: a must-have for IoT security and automation).
methodology_steps: For Vert: Deploy the Vert container on your server (e.g., using Docker). Access the web UI to upload files and select output format. For video conversion, also deploy Vertd (FFmpeg wrapper) on a machine with a GPU. Multiple conversions can run in parallel.
For BentoPDF: Run the BentoPDF container. Use the web interface to upload PDFs, then annotate, split, modify metadata, or edit content. All processing happens locally.

For Vaultwarden: Deploy the Vaultwarden Docker container. Configure a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx) with SSL. Use Bitwarden client apps (browser extension, mobile) to connect to your self-hosted instance. Import existing passwords or create new entries. Enable TOTP and passkey storage as needed.

For Syncthing: Install Syncthing on each device (Windows, macOS, Linux, etc.). Configure shared folders and set sync direction (send/receive). Adjust file versioning and conflict resolution settings. For backups, use Rsync or Kopia instead.

For SearXNG: Deploy the SearXNG container. Configure which search engines to include (e.g., Google, DuckDuckGo, Bing). Optionally integrate with Open WebUI by setting the search URL in Open WebUI's settings to point to your SearXNG instance.

For Home Assistant: Install Home Assistant OS or container on a dedicated device (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NUC). Add integrations for your smart devices (lights, sensors, cameras). Create automations using the visual editor or YAML. Ensure all devices are on a local VLAN to minimize cloud exposure.
conclusions: Self-hosting these six tools (Vert, BentoPDF, Vaultwarden, Syncthing, SearXNG, Home Assistant) provides a robust privacy-respecting alternative to common cloud services, eliminating data harvesting and ads. The article emphasizes that each tool addresses a specific privacy pain point, and together they form a comprehensive stack for daily computing needs.

The author notes that while self-hosting requires initial setup and maintenance, the privacy benefits outweigh the effort. For example, Vaultwarden's offline client backups mitigate the risk of server failure, and SearXNG's integration with local LLMs enables a fully open-source workflow.

Additional tools like Nextcloud, Jellyfin, Immich, Firefly III, Paperless-ngx, and Cryptomator are recommended for users who want to extend their self-hosted ecosystem further, covering cloud storage, media management, photo backup, finance tracking, document management, and client-side encryption.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in cybersecurity, homelabbing, and productivity. As a Red Teamer, you can leverage Vaultwarden to manage credentials for testing environments and SearXNG for private reconnaissance. For your homelab, Syncthing and Home Assistant offer practical automation and file syncing capabilities, while Vert and BentoPDF provide local, privacy-friendly utilities for file conversion and PDF editing—useful for both student work and technical documentation. The article also introduces additional tools like Immich and Paperless-ngx that can enhance your self-hosted infrastructure. Overall, it gives you a curated list of FOSS tools to build a privacy-centric digital environment, with actionable deployment steps.
extra_advice: Consider integrating SearXNG with a local LLM (e.g., Ollama + Open WebUI) to create a private AI assistant that can search the web without leaking your queries. This setup is particularly valuable for Red Teaming research, as it prevents search engines from profiling your investigation patterns. Also, pair Vaultwarden with a hardware security key (e.g., YubiKey) for 2FA on your self-hosted services to further harden your infrastructure.
